Integration, the VLSI Journal  
journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/vlsi  
Machine learning application for cell delay accuracy improvement at  
post-placement stage: A case study for combinational cells  
∗
Yassine Attaoui a, , Mohamed Chentouf a, Zine El Abidine Alaoui Ismaili b, Aimad El Mourabit c  
a Siemens EDA/CSD Calypto - Synthesis Solutions, Rabat, Morocco  
b Information, Communication and Embedded Systems (ICES) Team, Mohammed V University, Rabat, Morocco  
c System & Data Engineering Team, ENSA of Tangier, Abdelmalek Essaâdi University, Tangier, Morocco  
A R T I C L E I N F O  
A B S T R A C T  
Keywords:  
In the early VLSI design stages, as most interest is given to timing closure, various techniques are deployed to  
satisfy the timing constraints and close design timing. Nevertheless, timing still needs to be accurate at the early  
stages due to the lack of physical design information. Therefore, the challenge for most EDA Logic Synthesis  
engines is to improve the overall timing accuracy at higher levels of abstraction. In this paper, we shall reduce  
the error gap in Cell Delay estimation for a placed gate-level netlist. We will generate the post-placed netlist  
from two different Siemens EDA tools. The purpose is to improve the accuracy of the cell delays of Oasys-RTL  
Synthesis tool, based on accurate cell delays from Aprisa PnR. We have trained and tested various Machine  
Learning models on several 16 nm designs. We achieved the highest accuracy using the Random Forest model,  
where the average accuracy is 91.25%. Furthermore, as the accuracy of the Cell delay is improved, the average  
Root-Mean-Square-Error (RMSE) is reduced from 16.958 to 4.469.  
Delay estimation  
Machine learning  
Timing accuracy  
Regression  
Cell delay  
1. Introduction  
steps and the time-to-market factor, as it will reduce the iterative  
process on ASIC design, especially during placement [3].  
Nowadays, VLSI systems are increasingly complex, and the timing  
closure evolves as a challenge after each step of the development  
flow. The circuit’s timing closure at a high level of abstraction has  
always been the focus of all EDA companies after ensuring the circuit’s  
functionality. To make sure the design timing behavior is within the  
timing budget and meets all timing constraints at a defined operat-  
ing frequency, it is mandatory that EDA tools first estimate accurate  
cell and interconnect delays. This study will focus on the Logic Syn-  
thesis stage, where most EDA tools misestimate cell and net delays  
at Post-Synthesized gate-level netlist. Usually, path delays are either  
overestimated or underestimated compared to the post-placed netlist  
generated from a powerful P&R tool. This inaccuracy in the cell and  
interconnect delays reduces by advancing in the physical design flow,  
where the layout information is no longer estimated.  
The Oasys-RTL performs high-level timing optimization and itera-  
tive logic optimizations to generate an optimized placed netlist that  
meets the timing and area budgets. A third-party PnR tool can use the  
Oasys-generated timing-aware placed netlist for further physical design  
steps. Oasys-RTL uses NLDM extraction to estimate standard cell delay,  
along with the wire–load model for net delay estimation [1,2]. The  
tool occasionally miscalculates cell delays, and the average error gap  
between the Aprisa final and Oasys post-placed netlists remains slightly  
higher. More accuracy is achieved when the SPEF file with accurate  
RC parasitics values is provided. However, generating a netlist using  
an already generated SPEF file can take several days or weeks due to  
the SPEF generation and the design runtime, which depends on the size  
and architecture of the design.  
In this paper, we will attempt to improve Oasys-RTL Synthesis tool  
(Siemens EDA) [1] cell delay accuracy with the help of Machine Learn-  
ing algorithms. We will perform a prediction of each cell delay and  
compare it with the golden equivalent cell delay using Regression ML  
models. For this purpose, we considered the same cell delays extracted  
from Aprisa PnR (Siemens EDA) [2] as golden data. We intend to im-  
prove the timing accuracy at a preliminary stage at the post-synthesized  
gate-level netlist. This task will greatly impact subsequent design flow  
Recently, Machine learning (ML) applications are increasingly in-  
volved in VLSI CAD and ASIC design, and various EDA-based companies  
are adopting ML for different development purposes. Nonetheless, ML  
still needs to be fully exploited in the VLSI environment. In addition,  
the amount of data within the development flow continuously increases  
as we go down the flow, where additional layout and physical informa-  
tion evolve. Therefore, ML is a promising subject as we shall use it to

Y. Attaoui et al.  
Integration 90 (2023) 261–270  
without needing a SPEF file spefless flow, which has achieved good  
accuracy and reduced error on switching power.  
In [9], the author proposes a predictive machine learning model for  
PBA (Path-Based timing Analysis) from GBA (Graph-Based timing Anal-  
ysis). The model reduces the gap of PBA-GBA divergence consisting of  
arrival time and slack accuracy. Furthermore, since cell delay estimated  
using GBA-mode is more pessimistic than PBA-mode, the model arrives  
to reduce the PBA-GBA timing pessimism within a reduced runtime  
relative to ordinary PBA-mode. The model has achieved a well timing–  
runtime tradeoff with reduced predictive arrival time divergence and  
lower runtime.  
The author in [10] leverages the timing uncertainty at the pre-  
routing stage by implementing the Random Forest algorithm. The  
model has largely reduced the pessimism on net delays with improved  
pre-routing net delay predictions. Our study aims to bridge the gap be-  
tween the pre and post-placement stages by utilizing machine learning  
techniques. Our approach also extends the accuracy of the design into  
the early stages of the design process, precisely the Synthesis stage.  
Unlike [10], which utilizes small designs with a maximum of 1400  
flip-flops, our approach addresses the real challenges and difficulties  
encountered in actual System-on-Chip (SoC) implementations.  
A similar approach is conducted in [11] to improve timing accuracy  
at the pre-route stage. The author proposed a GNN-based (Graph Neural  
Network) machine learning framework to correct the arrival time and  
slack at endpoints. The timing engine GNN-based model has reached  
accurate pre-routing net delay and pin arrival time within a short  
inference time compared to the complete routing and STA flow. The  
authors worked on small and medium-sized designs and predicted  
routing impact on timing to guide the placement at the physical im-  
plementation. However, the feature set used for prediction was kept  
minimal.  
Fig. 1. ML-based Oasys-RTL metrics improvement.  
explore the circuit dataset and reveal some features, correlations, and  
dependencies, hence improving the tool’s QoR.  
Our primary concern is applying various ML models to improve  
cells’ delay for Oasys-RTL, considering the extracted cells’ delay from  
Aprisa P&R. Our approach consists of running 5 Regression algorithms  
on a set of designs. We will train the ML models to reduce the gap  
error on cell delays and test and evaluate their accuracy and error rate  
afterward. We have taken much care to keep similar post-placed netlists  
for both tools, while each tool performs placement and optimizations  
differently. First, the Oasys Virtual-Netlist is generated and then fed to  
Aprisa for further optimizations. In the meantime, Oasys will perform  
its optimizations to generate Oasys Final Netlist. Finally, cell delays will  
be extracted from the final Oasys netlist and Aprisa post-placed netlist  
as in Fig. 1.  
[12] proposes an ML-based routing-free crosstalk prediction frame-  
work. The model classifies nets that may have large coupling capac-  
itance or crosstalk noise. The authors predicted routing impact on  
timing to guide the placement at the physical implementation. In  
addition to improving timing at the routing stage, the authors also  
utilized small-sized designs, and few features were extracted.  
We have split the remainder of this paper into four major sections.  
Section 1 provides state of the art on ML applications on VLSI de-  
sign and related works. Section 2 presents the feature extraction and  
provides an overview of the dataset. Next, Section 3 gives some ML  
background, how we proceed with features and data processing, model  
training, testing, and evaluating. Finally, in Section 5, we present the  
experimental results and conclude afterward.  
In [13], the author tries to minimize the divergence in path slack  
between an internal and a signoff STA tool. The model developed does  
not include cell delay miscorrelations. However, in [14], the author  
developed a learning-based approach to correlate the timing divergence  
for path slack, setup time, stage, cell, and wire delays. The experiment  
is conducted on 45 nm and 28 nm. The model corrects slack divergence  
that reaches a reduction of up to 7 times.  
2. Related work  
Recently, Machine Learning applications have been increasingly  
growing in VLSI. Various EDA and IC design companies are exploring  
ML approaches that reduce the time-to-market and manufacturing run-  
time while assuring IC functionality. As a result, numerous applications  
are used at different design stages and using diverse approaches. This  
section studies various state-of-the-art ML applications related to VLSI  
Power, Performance, Area (PPA), and routing improvement.  
This work introduces a machine learning approach to pre-place  
timing improvement of a synthesis tool. The delay predictions reach an  
accuracy near the post-place timing delay of a physical-aware P&R tool.  
We largely avoid pessimism by using ML to extract knowledge from  
a physical-aware timing engine. Our main idea is to decompose the  
path delay into a cell and net delay and focus on training the model to  
predict solely combinational cell delays. The predictions are timing-arc  
based, and the model predicts based on the cell unateness. We trained  
the model taking into account the timing_sense attribute. Our method  
significantly advances over previous works as it closes the gap between  
pre- and post-placement, taking accuracy further to the earlier Synthe-  
sis stage. As designs are getting more complex with billions of gates in  
a single SoC, the development and fabrication costs are increasing, and  
the PPA target is pushed to the limits. Improving the timing accuracy  
at upper development stages will help meet the PPA target without re-  
spine, reducing the development cost and time to market. Additionally,  
this work utilizes larger and real industrial technology node designs.  
Finally, while implementing various ML models, we extracted a large  
number of cell attributes around 101 features. As a result, the proposed  
model reaches considerable delay prediction accuracy on advanced  
technology nodes. The experimental results show that our approach can  
obtain a 91.25% delay accuracy and 4.469 Root-Mean-Squared-Error.  
[4] presents a practical ML application for area prediction from a  
high-level specification of RTL components. The model achieved a high  
accuracy regardless of lacking netlist information at the specification  
level. From the HLS stage, [5] implemented a model to predict routing  
congestion. The routing bottlenecks were determined at early stages  
and resolved from the RTL level. In [6], the author tries to review  
power-grid-related issues such as IR drop and Electromigration (EM)  
using ML approaches. It implements a Machine Learning model that  
predicts the EM aging of the power grid network, as well as predicts a  
reliable power grid network by predicting the widths of metal lines. In  
terms of power analysis, [7] suggested an ML model that achieves high  
power estimation accuracy for RTL blocks across different benchmark  
designs. Compared to commercial gate-level power analysis tools, the  
author’s model reached satisfying accuracy and performance. In [8],  
the author suggested an ML model that improves the switching power  
accuracy. Various runs are conducted using (SPEF) file back-annotated  
RC parasitics. The trained model predicts the cell’s switching power

• The model’s predictions are timing-arc based for post-placed com-  
binational cells. In addition, the Models are trained to predict cell  
rise delay and fall delay separately.  
• The ML model has potentially reduced the delay uncertainty gap  
with an average accuracy of 91.25%. Furthermore, the model  
predicts within a short inference time.  
and net properties (Algorithm 2). The objective here is to improve the  
timing estimation at the synthesis phase. We need minimal changes in  
the Aprisa placed netlist to measure the timing inaccuracy induced by  
the lack of physical parameters, such as wire length, net resistivity, and  
capacitance. We will tune the synthesis STA engine to correlate with  
the PnR STA estimations. Thus, Oasys sees and optimizes real timing  
violations, improving flow convergence and runtime at the physical  
design step. This objective requires the PnR to place and globally route  
without optimizing the design, then estimate the timing by the PnR  
static timing analysis engine and measure the differences compared to  
synthesis reported values. Hence, we called the core placement engine  
of Aprisa with low timing effort. We aim to integrate the trained model  
in Oasys-RTL environment. Therefore we avoid Aprisa features and  
backend attributes dependencies during model training.  
• The proposed model exhibits good generalization performance,  
and its accuracy can be further improved by incorporating ad-  
ditional training data and tuning hyperparameters.  
The ML application on VLSI design is eventually a raw subject,  
and additional research efforts can be conducted in terms of QoR  
enhancement [15]. In the next section, we present our intent in ap-  
plying ML to enhance the cell delay accuracy for Oasys-RTL gate-level  
netlist. The tool’s inaccuracy at the logic synthesis stage is mainly due  
to the lack of physical information. This leads to challenging timing  
closure at high abstraction levels, and timing-critical paths may reside  
violated due to miscalculations. Moreover, the tool’s inaccuracy may  
lead to a false violation or a false non-violated path. In this trial, we  
attempt to improve the accuracy of cell delays by feeding-back physical  
information to our ML models as in Fig. 1.  
Algorithm 1 Oasys-RTL Frontend flow.  
3. Designs characterization  
1: Import design data  
2: Set 0-RC parameter  
This section presents the flow used to generate the Oasys-RTL  
final netlist and the Aprisa placed netlist. We also briefly describe the  
implemented designs and an overview of the used ML algorithms.  
We have performed two separate runs using five multi-VT industrial  
designs of different sizes as presented in Table 1. The first run mainly  
uses Oasys-RTL synthesis tool to generate a post-place netlist. The  
second run uses Aprisa PnR to generate a similarly optimized netlist.  
We summarize designs’ information in Table 1. The total number of  
standard cells reflects the size of each design. The extracted data is split  
into training and testing datasets for TC1 to TC4, while we used TC5  
entirely for testing purposes.  
3: Set design timing constraints  
4: Import floorplan  
5: Synthesize design, map to technology cells  
6: Perform Virtual optimizations  
7: Write Virtual Netlist  
8: Optimize timing paths groups  
9: Perform Placement  
10: Perform post-placement opt.  
11: Write Final Netlist  
12: Report cells attributes  
13: Report nets attributes  
As the clock network at this stage is ideal clock, the slew at the  
Flip-Flop inputs is considered null compared to the combinational  
cell’s inputs. Mixing sequential and combinational timing arcs will not  
help the ML models train and learn. We have split the problem into  
two categories Sequential and Combinational, as they have different  
properties. We focused on Combinational cells delay, while the sequential  
cells delay will be subject to another study. In addition to sequential  
cells, net delays are discarded and will be studied in a separate paper.  
We instructed Oasys-RTL and Aprisa to run using 0-RC and 0-LOAD pa-  
rameters consecutively, which will consider nets as ideal interconnects  
with zero delays.  
14: Report libraryCells attributes  
15: Report Cells delays and QoR metrics  
Algorithm 2 APRISA Backend flow.  
1: Import design data  
2: Set config timing 0-LOAD mode  
3: Set design timing constraints  
4: Import floorplan  
5: Import Oasys-RTL Virtual Netlist  
6: Perform global and detailed placement  
7: Perform global route  
The flow commands presented in Algorithm 1 and Algorithm 2  
set design initial setup files, logical and physical libraries, process  
technology libraries, design constraints, and a predefined Floorplan.  
Afterward, the logic is mapped to technology cells and timing-aware  
area optimized. The timing paths are then defined and optimized. This  
step is iterable until optimizing all possible timing violated paths (few  
paths may remain violated). Then, we instructed Oasys-RTL to generate  
a Virtual Netlist. Finally, the tool performs a timing-aware placement  
to generate a final netlist for further backend process [1,2]. Next, we  
collected the designs’ data in CSV files. The CSV files contain the cell’s  
and net’s and library cell’s attributes, corresponding cell delays, and  
QoR metrics for all possible design timing arcs (Algorithm 1).  
On the Aprisa side, we have used the already generated Oasys-RTL  
Virtual Netlist as a starting netlist. Afterward, we instruct the tool to  
place the design with low effort to avoid changing the netlist. Finally,  
from the final post-placed netlist, we extracted QoR metrics and cell  
8: Write post-placed Netlist  
9: Report cells attributes  
10: Report nets attributes  
11: Report libCells attributes  
12: Report cells delays and QoR metrics  
4. Machine learning algorithms and dataset  
Machine Learning allows the user to recognize pattern correlations  
and features’ trends within complex data structures. This trial aims to  
apply ML to reveal the patterns between the extracted cell delay and  
other cell attributes. As Backend data continuously increases, manually  
revealing the features correlations by human observation becomes  
almost unfeasible. We assume the predicted data is a continuous numer-  
ical float for cell delay prediction. Therefore we require a SUPERVISED

for Random Forest and Decision Tree, the feature elimination is done  
automatically [18–20].

The next step is the feature encoding, otherwise converting cate-  
gorical columns of a class object into numerical or boolean columns.  
The gathered datasets contain diverse categorical input variables. For  
instance, Arc Sense and Arc Type columns take a few states solely and  
should be treated as enumerate type. Therefore we converted them into  
boolean type by creating new columns. The number of added dummy  
columns depends on the number of states.

Regression model. Various state-of-art Regression models were studied.  
The chosen algorithms are Linear Regression, Decision Tree, Random  
Forest, Support Vector Regression, and K-Nearest Neighbors. The role of  
data in ML is crucial, and the quality of data is the key to improving  
the model’s accuracy. Consequently, we spent considerable time on  
the dataset processing. This section provides an overview of the used  
models, data processing, and model evaluation.  
As a result, the dataframes after pre-processing have kept integers,  
boolean and floating types, with no outlier and all categorical types  
converted into boolean columns.  
4.2. Model training  
The shortlisted models were adapted and fitted to our datasets  
using Python Scikitlearn library [18]. In this chapter, we give a slight  
background of each model.  
4.1. Data processing  
In this chapter, we present different data pre-processing steps. We  
collected data by generating a collection of CSV files as in the flow  
in Algorithm 1 and Algorithm 2. Then, the CSV files corresponding to  
cell and net properties, cell-net connections, library cell attributes, cell  
delays, and QoR metrics were merged into one master CSV using inner  
juncture on the cell full name, from pin and to pin columns. Where the  
full name, from pin and to pin are specified as data-frame indexes (ID) of  
the master CSV and identify each timing arc. During data preparation,  
we dropped several columns from the master CSV due to their weak  
informative contribution to the prediction. In the whole pre-processing  
step, the Scikit-learn Pandas library [16,17] is used to manage the  
master CSV file as a Dataframe that represents all valid design timing  
arcs. Table 3 summarizes the Dataframe’s information used for the  
training and testing datasets. Every row of the Dataframe represents a  
timing arc, and every column is an attribute of the corresponding cell.  
As Aprisa golden data divides timing arcs into positive and nega-  
tive unate, referred to as cell delay rise and cell delay fall attributes.  
Consequently, we trained the ML models first to predict delay rise  
attribute, which refers to positive unate timing arcs. Then, we carried  
a similar approach to predict delay fall. This paper contains the result  
accomplished on cell delay rise prediction, while delay fall prediction  
follows a similar approach.  
Multi-Linear Regression: The MLR is also known as multiple regres-  
sion, which is an extension of single linear regression. This algorithm  
predicts continuous variables from multiple linear combinations of  
input variables. The algorithm defines a set of coefficients (weights)  
for each input variable. Then, it keeps correcting the coefficients based  
on the resulting accuracy of the predicted output variable [18,21].  
Decision Tree: The DT is a non-linear algorithm that uses a tree  
model of decisions. DT is also used as regression to predict continuous  
data and is remarkably powerful that can fit complex datasets. The  
tree of decisions contains nodes and leaves, the nodes are tests on the  
attributes, and the leaves are the results of the tests [16,18,22].  
Random Forest: Also known as the Random Decision Forests, the  
RF is one of the powerful algorithms for data prediction. The RF is  
constructed from multiple decision trees, and the resulting outputs of  
each decision tree will help the RF predict the output variable value.  
Using multiple trees makes RF a well-fitting algorithm for most kinds  
of datasets [18,22].  
Support Vector Regression: The regression part of the Support Vector  
Machine (SVM) can also be used to predict continuous data. Its mission  
is to approximate a large number of data to a linear function. However,  
the difference from linear regression is that the SVR sets a threshold er-  
ror allowance around the function line, which authorizes and considers  
points within the threshold margins [18].  
As part of data processing, outlier removal is a preliminary phase  
in data preparation. Outliers are anomalies in the dataset caused by  
the tool’s miscalculations due to the lack of information or errors while  
manipulating data. We started detecting outliers by plotting a scattered  
plot and identifying cells with massive output capacitance and slew  
differences. The cut-off using Inter Quartile Ranger (IQR) approach at  
6-sigma has defined the upper and lower bounds from the mean value.  
We discarded only cells located beyond and below 6-sigma to drop the  
fewer possible outliers and keep data integrity while achieving higher  
accuracy. In Table 2, we summarize the outliers percentage per design.  
In Fig. 2, we display a scattered plotting after outlier removal, the ꢀ-  
axis is the Oasys cell delay, and ꢁ -axis is the Aprisa cell delay. We also  
highlight the output capacitance as a heatmap. Finally, these outlier  
timing arcs were dumped from the TC1, TC2, TC3, and TC4 dataframes.  
However, TC5 is considered new raw data, and no outlier was removed.  
We remarked that these outliers are High Fanout cells and will be  
treated in a separate paper using clustering algorithms.  
K-Nearest Neighbors: The KNN is a non-parametric algorithm also  
used for regression. The KNN’s purpose is to find similarities between  
the input and output variables. Then it selects the K closest inputs to  
build the non-linear function. The KNN weakness is the runtime, and  
the algorithm becomes slower while dealing with a large dataset [18].  
4.3. Model evaluation  
Once the model is fitted to the dataset and trained to predict new  
data, we evaluate the quality of predictions since the purpose is not  
only to build a model but to create a high-quality model with high  
predictive accuracy. We have chosen the Root-Mean-Squared-Error and  
Mean-Absolute-Error as metrics to measure the error rate of each model,  
and we used R2 score to evaluate how good the predictions are. We  
compared each model’s performance with these metrics and drew a  
conclusion in the next section [18].  
The feature selection is also a component of data preprocessing. Its  
purpose is to improve the model performance by reducing the number  
of input variables. The feature selection evaluates the correlation of  
each input variable with the outputs variable. The most contribut-  
ing input features are given a score depending on their correlation  
level. Features with higher scores are the most contributing to the  
output variable prediction. We performed a RFE (Recursive Feature  
Elimination) as a feature selection technique in this study. However,  
Root Mean Squared Error: the RMSE refers to the squared root of  
the sum of the squared distances between predicted and actual values  
(Eq. (1)) [18].

Mean Absolute Error: The MAE is the sum of all absolute error  
training–testing split for each design from TC1 to TC4 using the Python  
Scikit-learn function train_test_split. We trained each model using each  
design separately and extracted the resulting accuracy scores and error  
rates as presented in Table 5. Our objective was to observe the behavior  
of each model regarding different design architectures.

ꢆ
Afterward, we created the Bunch dataset by combining TC1, TC2,  
TC3, and TC4 into a single CSV file, which was then shuffled and split  
into a 75–25 training–validation split. The Bunch dataset is referred  
to as such in Tables 4 and 6. The validation set was used to identify  
the optimal hyperparameters that result in the highest model accuracy.  
This was accomplished by employing the GridSearchCV function from  
the scikit-learn library. To further evaluate the model’s generalization  
ability, the models trained on the Bunch dataset were tested on new un-  
seen data (TC5) to assess its generalization capability. Hence, TC5 was  
utilized solely for testing the models trained with the Bunch dataframe  
(Table 6). In Table 6, we present the R2 score and evaluation metrics  
corresponding to the validation set using the best estimators provided  
by the GridSearchCV function and scores obtained after predicting  
using TC5 data.  
R2 score: The coefficient of determination R2 describes the sum of  
squared residuals per sum of squares. The sum of squares is the distance  
from the actual and their average. While the residuals are the distance  
between the predicted and actual values. The R2 score is a percentage  
that indicates the predicted responses are good when R2 is closer to 1  
(Eq. (3)) [18].  
∑
ꢆ
ꢇ=1(ꢎꢌꢍꢏꢎꢐꢇ − ꢈꢉꢊꢋꢇꢌꢍꢊꢋꢇ)2  
ꢇ=1(ꢎꢌꢍꢏꢎꢐꢇ − ꢒꢊꢎꢆ)2  
ꢂ2 = 1 −  
(3)

5. Experimental results  
In this section, we present the results of each model after training  
and testing. We first compared Oasys’ initial delays versus the Aprisa  
golden delays (Fig. 2). Second, we plotted a graphical representation of  
the predicted cell delays versus Aprisa golden delays (Fig. 3). Finally,  
we analyzed the plots and identified the adequate model with the  
highest accuracy.  
5.2. Prediction results  
The target delays are predicted using Linear Regression, Decision Tree,  
Random Forest, K-Nearest Neighbors, and Support Vector Regression. In  
Fig. 3, we display the resulting predictions for the testing dataset per  
model and design. The Figure contains 20 subplots, five models per  
design. As a result, the predicted values look more consolidated and  
proximate to the golden value.  
5.1. Data analysis  
First, we visualized the initial Oasys versus Aprisa cell delays in  
scattered plotting, as pictured in Fig. 2. The ꢀ-axis is the initial state  
of Oasys cell delay, and the ꢁ -axis is the corresponding Aprisa cell  
delay. For a more reasonable interpretation, we comprised the cell  
output capacitance referred to as the heatmap color for each cell. The  
higher the capacitance is, the more the color is red. The scattered dots  
visualize a slight inaccuracy of Oasys-RTL in calculating cell delay.  
Next, the MEAN and STDEV of the Oasys cell delays are computed  
and displayed in the plot legend and tracked in Table 3. The initial  
Oasys MEAN and STDEV are 15.603 and 6.566, which are significantly  
distant from Aprisa MEAN and STDEV 25.793 and 16.264. Finally,  
we computed the RMSE error for Oasys cell delays, where the average  
initial Oasys RMSE is 16.958 (Table 3). Second, we employed a 75–25  
Table 5 summarizes each model’s accuracy R2 score and the ob-  
tained error metrics RMSE and MAE. We note that the models’ response  
differs from one design to another depending on the internal design  
architecture. The Random Forest predictions are the most accurate, with  
an average accuracy score of 91.25%. The Random Forest RMSE and  
MAE as well are the lowest average error with an RMSE and MAE  
of 4.469 and 1.9, respectively. With a very close accuracy and lower  
RMSE and MAE, the KNN model has the second least average errors  
of 4.941 and 2.144 and the second-highest accuracy score of 89.28%.  
The Decision Tree also offers a high accuracy score of 87.49% and  
average errors of 5.212 and 2.529. However, SVR and Linear Regression  
are considered inappropriate for our delay prediction, and both show

Table 7  
the lowest accuracy, highest RMSE, and MAE. We visualize scores and  
RMSE comparison in a histogram as in Figs. 5 and 6.

Similarly, we trained new models using the ’Bunch’ combined  
dataframe and used TC5 as testing data. The Random Forest model  
shows the highest accuracy of 89.12% using validation data and  
75.72% using testing data. The RF also provides the lowest errors rate  
of 6.29 and 2.731 for validation data, 6.947 and 3.099 for testing  
data. Both obtained scores are lower than the average line in Table 5,  
which is expected because TC5 is entirely new data and may contain  
outliers that significantly impact the model’s predictions. Consequently,  
the Random Forest model delivered higher stability and predictive  
capability, reaching a 75.72% accuracy for unseen data is mainly  
convenient. The accuracy of the model can be enhanced by feeding it  
with a larger training dataset and by further tuning the random forest  
with additional hyperparameter configurations. In Fig. 4, we plot the  
resulting predictions of the testing dataset TC5 per model in 5 subplots.  
As a result, the predicted values look more consolidated and proximate  
to the golden value.

Even the resulting scores achieved by the Random Forest, KNN, and  
Decision Tree are among the highest, we compared their training time  
and inference time. The Random Forest requires a few seconds to train  
and predict cell delays, depending on the design size. As in Table 7,  
TC4 lasted around 14 s to train and 1.7 s to predict results. In contrast,

average RMSE for cell delay predictions has decreased to 4.469, which  
is a significant improvement compared to the original Oasys average  
RMSE of 16.958. Additionally, the mean and standard deviation of  
the predicted cell delays are more aligned with those of the Aprisa,  
with the mean being 26.105 compared to 25.793 and the standard  
deviation being 15.184 compared to 16.264. Hence, we have achieved  
a more comparable mean and standard deviation. Similarly, for the  
Bunch dataset, the error has been significantly reduced from 20.069  
to 6.290, although it is slightly higher than the average. This pattern  
is also observed in TC5, where the RMSE error has been reduced from  
12.463 to 6.947. At the same time, the mean and standard deviation  
are closely aligned with those of the Aprisa.

Finally, we performed a feature selection on the testing data to  
spotlight the highly involved features during delay prediction. The  
model assign scores to each input feature based on its usefulness  
during the prediction phase. High-scored features are the most relevant  
and contribute the most to the prediction. We illustrate in Fig. 7 the  
selected features as per Random Forest. The features ranking indicates  
the following order: cell delay retains the highest weight (42%), then  
the output capacitance (28%), cell fanout (12%), input slew rise (5%)  
as well as the output slew rise (3%), these are considered the highly  
contributing features. However, the cell leakage and cell pins number also  
have a small contribution.  
SVR and KNN models last longer and attain a few minutes, exceeding  
an hour of inference time for the SVR model, which does not align with  
our end purpose. The Bunch dataframe took a few seconds to predict  
delays using LR and DT and less than a minute for RF, which perfectly  
matches our interest in using this model as a framework. As per the  
used machine, we have run all models using identical CPU and memory.  
5.3. Random forest and further analysis  
In Table 8, we present the mean, standard deviation, and RMSE  
of the predictions made by the Random Forest model. Notably, the

Training/Testing  
5.4. Training with reduced features  
of data, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Zine El  
Abidine Alaoui Ismaili: Conception and design of study, Acquisition  
of data, Analysis and/or interpretation of data, Writing – original draft,  
Writing – review & editing. Aimad El Mourabit: Conception and design  
of study, Acquisition of data, Analysis and/or interpretation of data,  
Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing.  
In light of the results obtained from the feature selection process,  
the Random Forest algorithm assigned high scores to the features of  
cell delay, output capacitance, cell fanout, input slew rise, and output  
slew rise. To further assess the impact of these high-scored features  
on the model’s performance, we reduced the Bunch dataset to include  
only these features, as shown in Table 9. The reduced dataset was  
then shuffled and split into a 75–25 training–testing split. Finally,  
new models were trained and evaluated. As presented in Table 9,  
the reduction of features led to a slight decrease in accuracy and  
increased RMSE. The accuracy of the Random Forest model dropped  
from 89.12% to 83.60%, which is still considered acceptable. On the  
other hand, the Support Vector Regression (SVR) model retained its  
accuracy of 84.89% and emerged as the best estimator model for the  
reduced dataset. Regarding runtime, the Random Forest algorithm took  
approximately 15 s to train, while the SVR runtime was significantly  
reduced from 9639 s to 530 s. We recognize the potential of using the  
SVR model with reduced datasets. This insight motivates us to tune new  
hyperparameter configurations for the SVR model, aiming to increase  
its accuracy further and reduce runtime.  
Declaration of competing interest  
The authors declare that they have no known competing finan-  
cial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to  
influence the work reported in this paper.  
Data availability  
Data will be made available on request.  
Acknowledgment  
All authors approved the version of the manuscript to be published.  
References  
6. Conclusion  
[1] Siemens EDA Oasys-RTL™ user’s guide, in: Oasys-RTL™ User’s Guide, Software  
Version 2021.2.R1, 2021.  
[2] Siemens EDA APRISA™ user’s guide, in: APRISA™ User’s Guide, Release Version  
2022, 2022.  
In this paper, we studied a Machine Learning application for cell  
delay prediction for a post-placed gate-level netlist. Our purpose is to  
improve the accuracy of cell delays at an early design stage. We trained  
and tested five Machine Learning models with the help of several  
industrial designs. We have improved Oasys cell delay accuracy based  
on Aprisa Backend information, which we consider golden data. The  
proposed ML models achieved a higher prediction accuracy and lower  
error rate. The Random Forest model had the highest accuracy and low-  
est RMSE. Consequently, we reduced the average RMSE from 16.958  
to 4.469 with an average accuracy of 91.25%. The Random Forest  
also produced higher stability and predictive capability for new design  
circuits. With more data added to train the Random Forest model, we  
can achieve an excellent generalization ability. The objective is to use  
this model integrated into a Python framework right after the logic  
synthesis stage. The model will provide precise estimations without  
the need for physical information. Based upon the selected features  
highlighted in the last chapter, the model will use only attributes within  
the Oasys synthesis tool. The model will support R&D and QA engineers  
to detect the tool’s irregularity and spotlight outliers engendered during  
logic synthesis. In terms of runtime, the Random Forest model delivers  
accurate values within a few seconds depending on design complexity,  
which perfectly matches our purpose. As a future work and extension  
to this study, we look forward to improving the training dataset with  
additional large designs and enhancing the overall model’s accuracy.  
We plan to investigate and implement other complex algorithms to  
enhance the model’s performance and integrate it into a comprehensive  
Python-based framework for wider application and accessibility.
